headline: Tata, Mahindra, and Hyundai Face a New Threat Nobody Expected
summary: India’s electric vehicle market has a new challenger, and it is arriving with aggressive pricing, local manufacturing, five-star safety ratings, and a rapidly expanding product strategy. VinFast’s early momentum is creating pressure on established players including Tata Motors, Mahindra, and Hyundai while forcing the industry to rethink how the next phase of EV competition will unfold.
seo_title: Tata, Mahindra, Hyundai Face VinFast EV Threat
meta_description: VinFast's rapid rise in India is challenging Tata, Mahindra and Hyundai with aggressive pricing, safety ratings and local EV production.

article_html:
Tata, Mahindra, and Hyundai Face a New Threat Nobody Expected

Summary: A new EV contender is challenging the established order through competitive pricing, local manufacturing, safety credentials, and ambitious expansion plans.

Introduction

For years, India's electric vehicle conversation has largely revolved around a handful of familiar names. Tata Motors built an early lead, Mahindra launched an aggressive new-generation EV strategy, and Hyundai positioned itself as a technology-focused alternative. Yet the most interesting development in the market may now be coming from outside India.

VinFast, the Vietnamese electric vehicle manufacturer, has entered the country with a strategy that combines local assembly, competitive pricing, strong safety credentials and an unusually broad ecosystem approach. While many new entrants struggle to gain visibility, VinFast appears to be targeting volume segments directly rather than spending years establishing premium credentials first.

What Happened

The company's first major push centers around the VF6 and VF7 electric SUVs. The VF6 enters one of the most contested EV segments in the country, competing against products from Tata, Mahindra, Hyundai and MG. Pricing has emerged as one of the biggest talking points because local assembly in Tamil Nadu has allowed VinFast to position the vehicle aggressively.

At the same time, both the VF6 and VF7 have achieved five-star Bharat NCAP safety ratings. In a market where safety is increasingly influencing purchase decisions, this immediately gives the brand credibility among Indian buyers.

The broader strategy extends beyond products. VinFast is building manufacturing capability, dealer networks, charging support and after-sales infrastructure simultaneously. The company has also introduced buyer confidence measures including assured value programs designed to reduce concerns around resale value.

Missing Details

Despite the positive headlines, several critical questions remain unanswered. Long-term service quality remains largely untested. Established manufacturers benefit from decades of network development, whereas new entrants must prove their ability to support customers over years of ownership.

Battery durability in Indian operating conditions is another area that requires longer-term observation. While warranty coverage appears competitive, real-world ownership data remains limited.

Charging network integration is also evolving. While the company has outlined ecosystem ambitions, buyers will ultimately judge convenience based on everyday usability rather than strategic announcements.

Contradictions and Open Questions

The available information presents a few notable contrasts. On one hand, official safety testing results demonstrate strong engineering capability. On the other hand, some road-test evaluations have highlighted concerns involving ADAS calibration, interface complexity and real-world efficiency.

Range claims also deserve careful examination. Claimed figures look competitive on paper, but independent testing suggests real-world results can vary significantly depending on conditions and usage patterns.

There are also differences in product positioning across various reports. Some descriptions emphasize affordability and accessibility, while others frame the vehicles as premium alternatives. The reality likely sits somewhere between those two narratives.

Buyer Advice

For consumers considering an EV purchase today, VinFast deserves serious consideration, but not blind enthusiasm.

Buyers prioritizing safety will find the five-star Bharat NCAP ratings particularly attractive. Those seeking modern styling, strong feature lists and competitive pricing may also discover compelling value.

However, prospective customers should evaluate dealership proximity, service infrastructure and charging convenience before making a decision. New brands can offer impressive specifications, but ownership experience ultimately determines long-term satisfaction.

Shoppers comparing alternatives should conduct back-to-back test drives against established competitors. Attention should be paid not only to acceleration and features but also to software usability, cabin ergonomics, ride quality and after-sales confidence.

Market Impact

The arrival of another credible EV manufacturer matters because India's electric transition is entering a more competitive phase. Early market leaders can no longer rely solely on first-mover advantages.

VinFast's pricing strategy has the potential to place pressure on margins throughout the industry. If consumers perceive equivalent safety, technology and range at lower prices, competitors may be forced to respond with incentives, feature upgrades or pricing adjustments.

The company's local manufacturing investment also sends an important signal. International EV players increasingly recognize India not merely as a sales destination but as a strategic production hub.

That trend could accelerate investment, supplier development and broader EV adoption.

Competitor Impact

Tata Motors: Tata remains the benchmark for scale in India's EV sector, but it now faces a challenger willing to compete aggressively on price and safety credentials. Maintaining leadership may require faster product refresh cycles and stronger differentiation.

Mahindra: Mahindra's new-generation EV lineup has generated strong enthusiasm. VinFast introduces additional competition precisely where Mahindra hopes to grow. The battle could become especially intense in electric SUV categories.

Hyundai: Hyundai has traditionally relied on technology, quality and brand trust. VinFast's combination of features and pricing creates pressure to justify premium positioning.

MG and Others: Smaller EV players may feel the impact even more sharply as buyer attention becomes increasingly fragmented.

Future Outlook

The next two years will determine whether VinFast becomes a lasting force or simply another ambitious entrant. Success depends on execution rather than announcements.

If the company can maintain competitive pricing, deliver reliable service support and continue expanding its ecosystem, it could establish itself as a meaningful player in the Indian market.

Failure to meet customer expectations, however, would quickly expose the challenges that have historically confronted new automotive brands.

Conclusion

India's EV market has reached an inflection point. VinFast's arrival introduces a new competitive dynamic that established manufacturers can no longer ignore. Strong safety ratings, attractive pricing, local manufacturing and ambitious growth plans give the company a credible foundation.

Whether that foundation translates into long-term success remains uncertain. What is clear is that Tata, Mahindra, Hyundai and every major EV player now face a competitor that few expected to emerge as quickly as it has.

FAQ

Why is VinFast attracting attention in India?

Its combination of pricing, safety ratings, local assembly and feature-rich products has made it one of the most closely watched EV entrants.

Which vehicles has VinFast launched?

The company has launched the VF6 and VF7 electric SUVs.

Are the vehicles safe?

Both models have achieved five-star Bharat NCAP ratings.

Who are the main rivals?

Key competitors include Tata, Mahindra, Hyundai, MG and other electric SUV manufacturers.

What is the biggest challenge for VinFast?

Building long-term customer confidence through service quality, infrastructure and ownership experience.

research.title: VinFast VF 6 and VF 7 Achieve 5-star Safety Certification From India's Bharat NCAP
research.description: PRESS RELEASE VINFAST VF 6 AND VF 7 ACHIEVE 5-STAR SAFETY CERTIFICATION FROM INDIA’S BHARAT NCAP
research.text_excerpt: VINFAST VF 6 AND VF 7 ACHIEVE 5-STAR SAFETY CERTIFICATION FROM INDIA’S BHARAT NCAP Mumbai, January 21st 2026 – VinFast VF 6 and VF 7 have been awarded the highest 5-star safety certification by India’s Bharat New Car Assessment Programme (Bharat NCAP). The results clearly demonstrate VinFast’s engineering capability in meeting stringent safety standards, while reaffirming the company’s customer-centric approach throughout product and service development. According to Bharat NCAP test results, both VF 6 and VF 7 achieved 5-star ratings in the two core safety categories. Specifically, VF 6 recorded 27.13/32 points in Adult Occupant Protection (AOP) and 44.41/49 points in Child Occupant Protection (COP). VF 7 achieved 28.54/32 points in AOP and 45.25/49 points in COP. These scores reflect a high level of protection for both adult and child occupants across both models. In the crash test assessments, the two VinFast models continued to demonstrate strong safety performance. In frontal impact tests, head & neck and upper leg protection scores for both the driver and passenger in VF 6 and VF 7 reached 4.000 points, the maximum on the Bharat NCAP scale. In side impact tests, both vehicles achieved full scores of 16.000/16.000 for adult occupants and 8.000/8.000 for child occupants. In the pole side impact test, the head injury assessment values for VF 6 and VF 7 were recorded at 109 and 131 respectively, significantly lower than the Bharat NCAP critical threshold of 700. These results indicate effective head protection in high-risk crash scenarios. Bharat NCAP is a vehicle safety assessment programme implemented by the Government of India, aligned with international practices such as Global NCAP. The programme evaluates occupant protection through a range of crash tests and awards safety ratings from 3 to 5 stars. Achieving the highest rating confirms that VF 6 and VF 7 meet the rigorous safety standards currently applied in the Indian market. Previously, VinFast models have also received positive recognition from regional safety assessment organisations, reflecting the company’s consistent focus on safety. Notably, the VinFast VF 8 achieved a 5-star ASEAN NCAP rating and surpassed more than 15 major automakers to secure 5 out of 6 safety awards from the organisation in 2024. Mr. Tapan Ghosh, CEO of VinFast India, said: “The 5-star safety ratings awarded to VF 6 and VF 7 by Bharat NCAP mark an important milestone, underscoring the quality and high safety standards of VinFast’s electric vehicles in India, one of the world’s largest automotive markets. These results show that VinFast EVs are built on a robust engineering foundation and meet stringent safety assessment requirements. We remain committed to continued investment in product improvement, delivering electric vehicles that are not only environmentally friendly and technologically advanced, but also provide customers with confidence and peace of mind throughout the journey toward electrified mobility.” Assembled at VinFast’s manufacturing facility in Tamil Nadu, VF 6 and VF 7 are premium electric SUVs designed for customers with high expectations for safety, technology, and everyday practicality. In addition to a 5-star Bharat NCAP-rated EV-dedicated platform, both models are equipped with 7 airbags and variant-dependent smart ADAS features, such as Auto Lane Changing Assist, Adaptive Cruise Control, Emergency Lane Keeping Assist, Front and Rear Automatic Emergency Brake, and Blind Spot Detection, enhancing collision avoidance capability and driving support across diverse traffic conditions. The two electric SUVs form an important part of VinFast’s comprehensive EV ecosystem in India, which encompasses local manufacturing, a retail network, charging infrastructure, and aftersales services. This integrated approach aims to deliver a seamless customer experience through strategic partnerships with local stakeholders. VinFast (NASDAQ: VFS), a subsidiary of Vingroup JSC, one of Vietnam’s largest conglomerates, is a pure-play electric vehicle (“EV”) manufacturer with the mission of making EVs accessible to everyone. VinFast’s product lineup today includes a wide range of electric SUVs, e-scooters, and e-buses. VinFast is currently embarking on its next growth phase through rapid expansion of its distribution and dealership network globally and increasing its manufacturing capacities with a focus on key markets across North America, Europe, the Middle East and Asia.

research.title: VinFast VF6 India price starts at Rs 16.49 lakh - Introduction | Autocar India
research.description: 2025 VinFast VF6 price in India starts at Rs 16.49 lakh; new electric midsize SUV rivals Curvv EV, BE 6, ZS EV, and Creta Electric. Tap to learn more.
research.text_excerpt: Vietnamese EV maker VinFast has officially commenced its India innings with the launch of the VF6 electric midsize SUV. The VF6’s price in India starts at Rs 16.49 lakh, placing it firmly among the likes of the Hyundai Creta Electric , MG ZS EV , Tata Curvv EV and Mahindra BE 6 . VinFast has been accepting bookings for the VF6 since mid-July for a token amount of Rs 21,000. VinFast VF6 price range in India is Rs 16.49-18.29 lakh The VF6 sports full-width LED DRLs and 18-inch alloy wheels Interior features 12.9-inch touchscreen, ventilated seats, panoramic glass roof, etc VF6 gets 59.6kWh battery pack and makes 204hp, 310Nm Locally assembling the VF6 in Tamil Nadu has allowed VinFast to competitively price the SUV. The VF6 is available in three trim levels, with the top-spec variant coming in at Rs 18.29 lakh. Here’s a full price breakdown of the VF6 line-up: On the outside, the VF6 is a distinctively styled SUV. Chief among its design flourishes is a pair of full-width LED DRL strips at the front and rear, which angle downwards to surround the VinFast emblems. The VF6 also gets generous plastic cladding all around, a sloping roofline and 18-inch alloy wheels. Coming to dimensions, the VF6 is 4,241mm long, 1,834mm wide and 1,580mm tall, with a 2,730mm wheelbase and 190mm ground clearance. Inside, the VF6 is a minimalist affair owing to the majority of in-car functions being integrated into the large 12.9-inch freestanding infotainment touchscreen. There isn’t even a digital driver’s display or instrument cluster of any kind, though the India-spec VF6 does get a heads-up display for that purpose. The VF6’s feature list is quite packed, with the SUV offering a panoramic glass roof, 8-way powered driver’s seat, cabin air filter, 8-speaker sound system, a wireless charger, ventilated front seats, dual-zone climate control, wireless Apple CarPlay and Android Auto, vegan leather upholstery, connected car tech, and more. Safety-wise, you get seven airbags as standard, Level 2 ADAS, auto park assist, automatic headlights, rain-sensing wipers, an electronic parking brake, and a 360-degree camera. The VF6 comes equipped with a 59.6kWh battery pack that’s wired up to a front-mounted electric motor. Powertrain output stands at 177hp and 250Nm in the entry-level VF6 Earth trim, while the higher-spec Wind trim generates 204hp and 310Nm. VinFast claims an ARAI-certified range of 468km for the VF6 Earth, whereas the Wind trim achieves 463km, and the SUV can complete the 0-100kph sprint in 8.9 seconds. As for charging, 3.3kW and 7.2kW AC chargers are supported along with DC fast chargers, with the latter delivering a 10 to 70 percent charge in 25 minutes. Unlike the VF7, the VF6 gets a 7-year/1.6 lakh km warranty, though the battery pack has the same 10-year/2 lakh km warranty. Vinfast Minio Green compact EV design patent filed in India New Vinfast 7 seat SUV design patent filed in India

research.title: Vinfast VF7 road test review - Introduction | Autocar India
research.description: Our comprehensive road test review of the Vinfast VF7 takes into account its exterior design, interior space and comfort, equipment list, range and performance, etc. Tap here for the full review.
research.text_excerpt: Flush-type non-motorised door handles are inconvenient to operate. India-spec version rides on 245/50 R19 tyres. Vietnamese carmaker Vinfast was quick to establish its presence in India with its two offerings – the VF6 and VF7 – and, over the past few months, has secured a spot among the top five bestselling EV manufacturers in the country. Both crossovers contribute almost equally to volumes, and in a recent interaction, Vinfast India CEO Tapan Kumar Ghosh credited strong design appeal, competitive pricing and ownership schemes aimed at lowering total cost of ownership for driving demand. In this test, we examine the company’s flagship crossover in India, the VF7, which takes on rivals such as the Mahindra XEV 9e and Tata Harrier EV . It’s offered with two battery options – 59.8kWh and 70.8kWh. The smaller pack is paired with a single motor driving the front wheels, while the larger battery also gets a dual-motor, all-wheel-drive setup in higher variants. We put the top-spec dual-motor AWD version to the test to see how it performs in the real world. In terms of dimensions, the Vinfast VF7 measures 4,550mm in length, 1,893mm in width and 1,596mm in height, making it smaller than both the Mahindra XEV 9 and Tata Harrier EV. However, its 2,840mm wheelbase is longer than its Indian rivals. It adopts a more crossover-like stance with a drooping nose and a sloping roofline, and as a result, the VF7 doesn’t look butch or imposing, adjectives which can be used to describe the Tata and Mahindra EVs. That said, it’s full of interesting details. Up front, the V-shaped LED DRL – a common design element across Vinfast models – flanks the company’s logo. Gloss-black body cladding around the 19-inch black wheels and along the lower doors add to its crossover credentials, while the pinched C-pillar lends it a floating roof effect. The raked rear windscreen and roof spoiler look striking, and like the front, the LED light bar extends from the V logo at the rear. The VF7’s restrained luxury theme continues on the inside with a minimalistic cabin layout. First impressions are positive, with a richly appointed interior – the dashboard, door pads, seats and steering wheel are draped in vegan leather finished in a premium brown-and-black colour scheme. The touchscreen commands centre stage, with toggle switches beneath it for the drive selector. Underlining the car’s driver-centric focus, the screen is angled towards the driver, and the steering wheel and stalks appear reminiscent of certain BMW models. Some may find the steering diameter slightly small and the rim a touch too thick. Fit and finish impress, and even though several hard plastics are used, overall material quality feels good. Taller drivers may need to set the seat to its lowest position and the steering wheel to its highest, else the wheel tends to brush against your thigh due to the high cabin floor. The powered front seats are comfortable, but there’s no lumbar adjustment, and some may find the seat base cushioning a bit excessive. The back seat is spacious – headroom, knee room and legroom are adequate, but the raised floor means passengers will need to stretch their feet under the front seats for better comfort. The flat floor and bench make it reasonably accommodating for a third passenger. The high window line, however, eats into the cabin’s sense of airiness, especially in versions without the glass roof. In terms of practicality, the front centre console houses cupholders, USB ports, a deep cubby, centre armrest and essential physical controls such as hazard lights, door lock/unlock and infotainment volume. There’s also a large storage shelf beneath the console, and the glovebox is generously sized. The door pockets have 1-litre bottle holders, but they’re too slim to hold much else. At the rear, you get AC vents, USB ports, seatback pockets, a fold-down armrest with cupholders, adjustable head restraints and three-point seatbelts for all occupants. Boot space is rated at 537 litres (measured to the roof), and it can accommodate a fair amount of luggage. The rear seats can also be split-folded for additional space. However, the boot is quite shallow, and there’s neither a spare tyre nor a front trunk. The Vinfast VF7 has scored five stars in Bharat NCAP for both adult and child occupant protection. Safety kit includes seven airbags (including a driver knee airbag), ESP, hill-start assist and ABS with EBD. It also gets ADAS features such as adaptive cruise control, lane centering, lane keep assist, blind-spot monitoring and high beam assist, among others. However, during our tests, the ADAS system behaved inconsistently – adaptive cruise control didn’t always slow down at a safe distance, and lane centering occasionally allowed the car to drift over lane markings. ‘Infinity’ variants get a full-sized panoramic glass roof, which floods the cabin with light – and heat. Since there’s no factory-fitted sunshade, the cabin gets extremely hot during summer, forcing the air-con to work harder, which will affect range. Our test car featured an aftermarket manual sunshade, which lacked the finesse of an OEM solution. Users must rely on the 12.9-inch touchscreen for nearly every function, including mirror adjustments, climate control, drive modes, trip data, and even the speedometer. The interface itself is relatively straightforward and touch response is decent, but most functions require multiple presses, making it less intuitive. While it gets wireless Android Auto and Apple CarPlay, the 8-speaker audio system falls short of expectations at this price point. Other features include 19-inch alloys, auto LED headlamps and wipers, a 360-degree camera, head-up display, electronic parking brake with auto hold, wireless charging, auto-dimming inside mirror and a powered tailgate. One feature notably missing is powered pop-out door handles. The manual flush-type handles are very inconvenient to use. In its most powerful guise, the 70.8kWh battery powers two motors that produce a combined 354hp and 500Nm. That’s 41hp more than the Harrier EV AWD (though with 4Nm less torque), and crucially, the VF7 is 117kg lighter, which makes it feel noticeably sprightlier. It gets three drive modes – Eco, Normal and Sport – which alter part-throttle response, though full-throttle performance remains unchanged. The motors pull strongly throughout, and those who enjoy brisk acceleration will appreciate its sharp responses. The VF7 sprints from 0-100kph in just 6.03 seconds and goes on to a top speed of 178kph. Rolling acceleration is equally impressive – 20-80kph takes just 3.16 seconds, and 40-100kph is dispatched in 3.95 seconds. For the money, the VF7 AWD is among the most powerful and quickest EVs available. Refinement is generally good, with negligible motor whine filtering into the cabin. However, road and tyre noise become noticeable at highway speeds. Regenerative braking, though, is a bit peculiar. There are four modes – Off, Low, Medium and High – but deceleration in each feels relatively mild, and there’s no single-pedal mode. In practice, you’ll still need to use the brake pedal frequently, especially in Low and Medium settings. In our deceleration tests from 80-20kph, it took 636.14m/46.46s in Low, 308.11m/22.24s in Medium and 161.48m/11.73s in High. For comparison, the Harrier EV slows from 80-20kph in 130.64m/8.89s in its strongest setting, and 161.42m/10.40s in its mid setting – similar to the VF7’s strongest mode. There’s also no quick access to regen levels via paddle shifters; like most functions, they must be selected through the touchscreen. The brakes themselves inspire confidence. There’s a strong pedal bite, and this 2.2-tonner stopped from 80kph in an impressive 24.82 metres. Vinfast VF7 tested battery efficiency and range The smaller 59.6kWh battery pack is claimed to deliver 438km, while the larger 70.8kWh single-motor variant has a claimed range of 532km. The dual-motor version tested here has a claimed range of 510km. Interestingly, unlike the Harrier EV AWD, which gets a front induction motor and a permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) at the rear, the VF7 has a PMSM on both axles – these are great for performance and acceleration; however, it doesn’t have the same free-spinning, low-drag advantage of an asynchronous secondary motor. In our tests, however, the dual-motor version returned 5.10km/kWh in the city and 5.92km/kWh on the highway. Extrapolated, the city range works out to 361km, highway 419km, thus giving you a combined real-world range of 391km – well short of the company’s claim. We conducted the test in Eco mode; in the city, regen was set to ‘High’ with an average speed of 19.6kph, while on the highway, regen was set to ‘Low’ with an average speed of 55kph. The VF7 uses a 400V electrical architecture with claimed DC fast charging speeds of up to 110kW and a 10-70 percent charge time of 28 minutes. However, when connected to a 180kW charger, it peaked at 95.5kW

research.text_excerpt: VinFast is a Vietnamese company started in 2017 as a part of the Vingroup to manufacturer cars in Vietnam and sell them in global LHD and RHD markets. It broke ground for its factory in Chennai on February 2024 and is expected to start production in India by mid-2025. VinFast currently has 1 hatchback, 3 five-seat SUVs and one seven-seat SUV in its range. These include the VF3, VF6, VF8 and the VF9. It is expected to enter India with a top-down approach and thus we will get the fully loaded VF9 to establish the brand before bringing the smaller models. The VinFast cars that will come to India will offer large interior space with big range as their USPs. research.title: VinFast VF6 And VF7 Launched in India: Design, Prices, Interior, Exterior, And Rivals Detailed - ZigWheels
research.description: VinFast has finally launched the VF6 and VF7 SUVs on our shores. Here’s a closer look at all of its details!
research.text_excerpt: Vietnamese giant, VinFast, has officially entered India with the VF6 and VF7 being their first SUVs on our shores. The VF6 is a compact SUV, priced from Rs 16.49 lakh (ex-showroom), while the VF7 full-size SUV comes at a higher price tag of Rs 20.89 lakh (ex-showroom). Both these EVs are well-packaged, stylish, and targeted to a completely different buyer! So without further ado, let’s take a closer look at both of these EVs in detail… Before getting into the details, here’s a rundown of the variants and pricing… VF6 Price Variants Price (ex-showroom) Earth Rs 16.49 lakh Wind Rs 17.79 lakh Wind Infinity Rs 18.29 lakh The VF6 is available in 3 variants and one battery pack option, with prices ranging from Rs 16.49 lakh to Rs 18.29 lakh (ex-showroom). More of this has been covered later in the article. VF7 Price Variants 59.6 kWh 70.8 kWh Earth FWD Rs 20.89 lakh — Wind FWD Rs 23.49 lakh — Wind Infinity FWD Rs 23.99 lakh — Sky AWD — Rs 24.99 lakh Sky Infinity AWD — Rs 25.49 lakh As seen in the table above, prices for the VF7 range from Rs 20.89 lakh to Rs 25.49 lakh (ex-showroom). You get different drivetrain options and a bigger battery option as well. VinFast VF6 The VF6 is positioned as the entry-level VinFast, set to take on the popular compact electric SUVs. Its styling is unlike any of its competitors and brings in a fresh design in the compact SUV space. It has plenty of sporty design elements, combined with clean body lines for a modern and appealing look. It sports eyebrow-shaped LED DRLs that are connected via the VinFast logo. The aggressively styled bumpers further add a nice touch to the fascia. It sits on 18-inch dual-tone alloy wheels and gets thick body cladding on the wheel arches, running across the lower section of the SUV. The rear features connected LED taillights, a well-integrated spoiler, and a chunky bumper. The India-spec model will come with six colour options: Infinity Blanc, Crimson Red, Urban Mint, Jet Black, Desat Silver, and Zenith Grey. The VF6’s interior takes the minimalist design approach, similar to most modern EVs, including the Tesla Model Y. While the infotainment unit dominates the dashboard, you also get plenty of physical controls in the lower section of the dashboard. There is no driver display, and you instead get a head-up display showing you all the vital information. Soft-touch materials across the cabin add a sense of sophistication, and you get silver inserts to contrast the sporty all-black theme. The top features include a 12.9-inch touchscreen, a 360-degree camera, a head-up display, heated and ventilated front seats, a panoramic glass roof, 7 airbags, and Level-2 advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS). The VF6 gets a respectable 59.6 kWh battery pack, available across two trims. In the top-spec trim. The performance output varies depending on the trim you choose. The Eco version makes 177 PS and 250 Nm, while the Plus iteration generates 204 PS and 310 Nm. The VF6 gets fast charging capabilities, ensuring a 20-80 percent top-up in just 30 minutes with a DC charger. VinFast VF7 The VF7 has been positioned as the flagship model and will compete with mid-size electric SUVs. In comparison to the VF6, the VF7 is a larger alternative with more features and a more premium interior with plenty of high-end features. The VF7’s design is sporty and upbeat, but not very polarizing or in your face. It comes with signature VinFast styling that will catch attention on our roads, and with over 4.5 metres in length, it has the road presence too. Its proportions are squat and wide, and the stretched-out ‘V’ will remind you of the car’s brand. It sports 19-inch alloy wheels, which complement the overall styling really well. Design elements like flush door handles add a sense of premiumness, and the rear end’s sleek and sporty design with subtle chrome inserts round off the overall look. Like the VF6, the VF7 also boasts a clean and subtle interior that screams modernism. The sophisticated dual-tone theme, combined with plenty of premium leatherette materials, elevates the feel of the cabin. Moreover, there are well-executed storage spaces as well for your knick-knacks. The 537-litre boot space is something is something you would appreciate during airport visits or your IKEA runs. The key amenities include a 12.9-inch touchscreen, an 8-way powered driver’s seat, a head-up display, a panoramic sunroof, and ventilated front seats. Safety is taken care of by multiple airbags, an electronic parking brake, an auto-dimming rear-view mirror, and a 360-degree camera. It also gets level-2 ADAS features. The VF7 gets a single 70.8 kWh battery pack, available in FWD and AWD trims. The FWD system features a single motor that makes 201 PS and 310 Nm, while the AWD variant gets dual motors that churn out 354 PS and 500 Nm. The AWD version sprints from 0-100 kmph in just 5.8 seconds, which is great for a full-size SUV. Furthermore, you also get fast charging with speeds of up to 150 kW. The FWD has a claimed range of 532 km. Rivals The VinFast VF6 rivals the likes of Hyundai Creta Electric, Tata Curvv EV, Mahindra BE 6, and MG ZS EV. The VinFast VF7 competes against the likes of the Volvo EX30, BMW iX1 LWB, Hyundai Ioniq 5, and BYD Sealion 7. ZigSays… For a car, or a car brand, to find success in India is more complicated than it looks. India is no doubt a tough market to crack, and many things need to be correct for a new brand to succeed. The good thing is that both VF6 and VF7 have strong fundamentals and look promising. Their biggest strengths are modern and unique design, and feature-rich cabins that actually feel high-end. India’s growing EV infrastructure and rising interest in affordable electric SUVs could play in their favor. However, challenges like brand recognition, after-sales service, and strong rivals (Tata, MG, Hyundai, and Mahindra) stand in the way. It would all come down to whether VinFast can connect with the Indian buyer or not, something that BYD has very well managed to do. What do you think about the VinFast VF6 and VF7 Let us know in the comments section below. The VF6 is a compact SUV, priced from Rs 16.49 lakh (ex-showroom), while the VF7 full-size SUV comes at a higher price tag of Rs 20.89 lakh (ex-showroom). Both these EVs are well-packaged, stylish, and targeted to a completely different buyer!
